serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Jhinga Malay Curry is approximately 300 g, comprising king prawns in an almond-cream curry sauce, served as a main course alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Prawn curry (Indian-style, cooked) — estimated from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods and USDA FoodData Central (Shrimp, cooked; Almond; Cream)
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Jhinga Malay Curry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA. Values estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for prawn curry (cooked king prawns in spiced gravy) with USDA FoodData Central data for almond paste and cream to reflect the almond-cream sauce component. Energy and macros are weighted estimates for a dish comprising approximately 45% king prawns, 30% almond-cream sauce, 15% tomato-onion gravy base, and 10% oil and spices.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database (prawn/shrimp dishes, low-carbohydrate seafood curries)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Jhinga Malay Curry. GI estimated at 25 based on analogous low-carbohydrate seafood curry dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). The dish is dominated by protein (prawns) and fat (almond cream), with very low carbohydrate content (~3.5 g/100 g), placing it firmly in the low-GI category. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (25 × 10.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg/100 g; ~1260 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single 300 g restaurant serving provides approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index (~25)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only ~3.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a low estimated GI of 25, Jhinga Malay Curry has a negligible glycemic load (~2.6 per serving) and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol from prawns (~115 mg/100 g) and saturated fat from cream (~2.8 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: King prawns are naturally high in dietary cholesterol and the almond-cream sauce contributes saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 150–200 g and favour preparation with reduced cream to lower saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jhinga Malay Curry is an Indian restaurant dish featuring fresh king prawns (jhinga) cooked in a rich, mildly spiced almond-cream sauce. The name 'Malay' in this context refers to the Mughal-influenced 'malai' (cream) style of cooking rather than Malaysian cuisine, though the dish is also served in Singapore's Indian restaurants. The sauce is prepared by simmering onions, garlic, ginger, and tomatoes with aromatic spices such as cardamom, cinnamon, and cumin, then enriching the gravy with ground almond paste and fresh cream to create a velvety, mildly sweet, and nutty flavour profile. King prawns are added and cooked until tender, absorbing the fragrant sauce. The dish is typically served as a main course alongside steamed basmati rice, naan, or roti. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with very low carbohydrate content, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The almond component contributes heart-healthy monounsaturated fats and vitamin E. Sodium content is elevated due to restaurant preparation methods, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated to be low (~25), making it appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when paired with low-GI accompaniments. Prawns provide significant dietary cholesterol, which should be noted for cardiovascular disease management.
common_user_queries: Is Jhinga Malay Curry the same as Malai Prawn Curry?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Jhinga Malay Curry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN prawn curry data with USDA almond and cream component data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for low-carbohydrate seafood curry dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-based estimation methodology is defensible and the dish composition is well-characterised.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-based estimates; no single approved-source entry exactly matches Jhinga Malay Curry. Values should be verified against laboratory analysis or a future ICMR-NIN / HPB entry if one becomes available. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA component sources used for this estimation. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g/100 g reflecting trace amounts from dairy cream; this is a trace value consistent with natural dairy trans fats. 4. GI is set to 25 as an analogous estimate for a low-carbohydrate seafood curry; gi_category is low per the ≤55 threshold rule. 5. The dish name 'Malay Curry' in the Indian restaurant context refers to the Mughal malai (cream) cooking style and does not indicate Malaysian cuisine origin. 6. Halal status is assumed based on the dish composition (seafood, dairy, nuts, spices) with no pork or alcohol ingredients; verification with the specific restaurant is recommended. 7. Sodium estimate of 420 mg/100 g reflects typical restaurant-prepared Indian curry sodium levels per ICMR-NIN reference data.
